19	
20	## 1. Project Scope
21	
22	### What This Is
23	A game-level NBA win probability predictor. Given two teams playing tonight, the model outputs a calibrated win probability for each team. Results are displayed on a deployed dashboard.
24	
25	### What This Is Not
26	- Not a real-time system. Data is fetched manually by running a script.
27	- Not a betting tool. Model will not beat Vegas lines — that is expected and documented.
28	- Not a championship simulator. Predicts individual game outcomes, not series or season winners.
29	- Not an automated pipeline. No schedulers, no cron jobs, no webhooks.
30	
31	### Explicit Constraints
32	- Data refresh: manual. Run `fetch_games.py` before each prediction session.
33	- Model retraining: manual. Run `train.py` at the start of each new season.
34	- Infrastructure: minimal. CSV files for data, flat file model serialization, single FastAPI endpoint.
35	- Target users: portfolio reviewers and yourself.
36	
37	### Success Criteria
38	- [ ] Model produces calibrated win probabilities (Brier score < 0.25)
39	- [ ] Calibration curve shows probabilities are trustworthy
40	- [ ] Backtest against Vegas lines documented honestly
41	- [ ] Dashboard deployed and publicly accessible
42	- [ ] README explains methodology, limitations, and results clearly
43	
44	---
45	
46	## 2. Architecture & File Structure
47	
48	```
49	NBA-Championship-Predictor/
50	│
51	├── data/
52	│   ├── raw/

58	│

71	│   │

74	│   │   ├── evaluate.py            # Brier score, calibration curves, Vegas backtest

103	
104	## 3. Tech Stack
105	
106	| Layer | Tool | Why |
107	|---|---|---|
108	| Data fetching | `nba_api` | Unofficial but well-maintained Python wrapper for NBA Stats API |
109	| Data processing | `pandas` | Standard for tabular data manipulation |
110	| Modeling | `xgboost` | Fast, interpretable, industry-standard for tabular data |
111	| Calibration | `scikit-learn` (isotonic regression) | Corrects probability output to be statistically trustworthy |
112	| Interpretability | `shap` | Explains which features drive each prediction |
113	| API serving | `FastAPI` | Lightweight, async, auto-generates docs at /docs |
114	| Frontend | `Next.js` + `Tailwind` | You already know this stack |
115	| Deployment | `Railway` or `Render` | Free tier sufficient for this project |
116	| Version control | `git` + GitHub | Required. Commit after every meaningful milestone. |
117	
118	---
119	
120	## 4. Data Dictionary
121	
122	### games_raw.csv (from nba_api)
123	| Column | Type | Description |
124	|---|---|---|
125	| SEASON_ID | str | Prefixed: `2` = regular season, `4` = playoffs |
126	| TEAM_ID | int | NBA's internal team identifier |
127	| TEAM_ABBREVIATION | str | e.g. BOS, LAL |
128	| GAME_ID | str | Unique game identifier |
129	| GAME_DATE | str | Format: YYYY-MM-DD |
130	| MATCHUP | str | e.g. "BOS vs. DAL" or "BOS @ DAL" |
131	| WL | str | W or L |
132	| PTS, REB, AST, STL, BLK, TOV, FG_PCT, FG3_PCT, FT_PCT | float | Box score stats |
133	| PLUS_MINUS | float | Point differential for this team in this game |
134	
135	### Key Notes on Raw Data
136	- Each game appears **twice** — once per team. You must deduplicate into one row per game before modeling.
137	- `SEASON_ID` prefix `4` means playoffs. Filter these out of training or handle separately.
138	- `MATCHUP` with `vs.` = home team. `@` = away team. Use this to engineer home/away feature.
139

142	## 5. Milestone Roadmap
143	
144	### Week 1 — Data Pipeline ✅ IN PROGRESS
145	- [x] Install `nba_api`, `pandas`
146	- [x] Fetch historical games 2015-16 to 2024-25 → `games_raw.csv`
147	- [ ] Fetch current season 2025-26 → `games_current.csv`
148	- [ ] Write `clean.py` — handle nulls, normalize columns, deduplicate games, add home/away flag
149	- [ ] Verify: no duplicate GAME_IDs, WL column is complete, GAME_DATE parses correctly
150	
151	**Exit criteria:** `games_clean.csv` exists, loads without errors, has one row per team per game, no nulls in key columns.
152	
153	### Week 2 — Feature Engineering
154	- [ ] Write `features.py`
155	- [ ] Rolling 10-game averages per team (PTS, REB, AST, FG_PCT, PLUS_MINUS)
156	- [ ] Rest days feature (days since last game per team)
157	- [ ] Home/away binary flag
158	- [ ] Player availability flag (approximate from box score: did starters play? check MIN)
159	- [ ] Season-based train/val/test split:
160	  - Train: 2015-16 to 2021-22
161	  - Val: 2022-23
162	  - Test: 2023-24 to 2024-25
163	- [ ] **NEVER random split across games — this causes data leakage**
164	
165	**Exit criteria:** `features.csv` exists. Train/val/test row counts verified. No future data leaks into training set.
166	
167	### Week 3 — Baseline Model
168	- [ ] Write `train.py`
169	- [ ] Logistic regression baseline first (sets the floor)
170	- [ ] XGBoost model second
171	- [ ] Output: win probability per game, not just W/L classification
172	- [ ] Write `evaluate.py`
173	- [ ] Accuracy, Brier score, log loss on val set
174	- [ ] Compare vs naive baseline (always predict home team wins ~58% of the time)
175	
176	**Exit criteria:** XGBoost Brier score < 0.25 on val set. Model saved to `models/xgboost_model.pkl`.
177	
178	### Week 4 — Calibration & Evaluation
179	- [ ] Plot calibration curve (reliability diagram)
180	- [ ] Apply isotonic regression calibration
181	- [ ] Save calibrator to `models/calibrator.pkl`
182	- [ ] Backtest on 2023-24 playoff games vs Vegas lines
183	- [ ] Document results honestly in `docs/methodology.md` — including where model fails
184	
185	**Exit criteria:** Calibration curve plotted and saved. Backtest results written up. Model limitations documented.
186	
187	### Week 5 — SHAP Feature Importance
188	- [ ] Install `shap`
189	- [ ] Generate SHAP summary plot — which features matter most?
190	- [ ] Generate SHAP waterfall plot for one example prediction
191	- [ ] Write up 3-sentence explanation of what the model learned in `docs/methodology.md`
192	
193	**Exit criteria:** Can explain in plain English why the model predicted a specific game outcome.
194	
195	### Week 6 — FastAPI Serving Layer
196	- [ ] Write `src/api/main.py`
197	- [ ] POST `/predict` endpoint: takes two team abbreviations + game date, returns win probabilities
198	- [ ] GET `/health` endpoint: returns last data refresh date + model version
199	- [ ] Load model and calibrator from disk at startup
200	- [ ] Add "last updated" timestamp to every response
201	- [ ] Test locally with curl or Postman before deploying
202	
203	**Exit criteria:** `/predict` returns valid probabilities that sum to 1.0. Deployed to Railway or Render.
204	
205	### Week 7 — Dashboard
206	- [ ] Next.js project scaffolded in `/frontend`
207	- [ ] `GameCard` component: shows team names, win probabilities, confidence
208	- [ ] `CalibrationChart` component: shows calibration curve
209	- [ ] "Last updated" timestamp visible on every page
210	- [ ] Connects to deployed FastAPI endpoint
211	
212	**Exit criteria:** Dashboard publicly accessible. Shows current playoff matchup with win probabilities.
213	
214	### Week 8 — Polish & Documentation
215	- [ ] Write `README.md`: what it is, how it works, results, limitations, how to run locally
216	- [ ] Write `docs/methodology.md`: full model writeup
217	- [ ] Record 2-minute demo video
218	- [ ] Clean up all `print` statements → use `logging`
219	- [ ] Final commit with clean git history

223	## 6. Implementation Guide
224	
225	### Running the project end-to-end (for any future session or collaborator)
226	
227	```bash
228	# 1. Set up environment
229	python -m venv .venv
230	source .venv/bin/activate
231	pip install -r requirements.txt
232	
233	# 2. Fetch data (run manually when you want fresh data)
234	python src/data/fetch_games.py        # historical
235	python src/data/fetch_current.py      # current season
236	
237	# 3. Clean data
238	python src/data/clean.py
239	
240	# 4. Engineer features
241	python src/data/features.py
242	
243	# 5. Train model
244	python src/model/train.py
245	
246	# 6. Evaluate model
247	python src/model/evaluate.py
248	
249	# 7. Run API locally
250	uvicorn src.api.main:app --reload
251	
252	# 8. Run frontend locally
253	cd frontend && npm run dev
254	```
255	
256	### How to make a prediction
257	```bash
258	curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/predict \
259	  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
260	  -d '{"home_team": "BOS", "away_team": "OKC", "game_date": "2026-06-06"}'
261	```
262

264	
265	## 7. Known Bugs & Gotchas
266	
267	These are confirmed issues you will hit. Read before starting each phase.
268	
269	### Data Layer
270	| # | Issue | Impact | Fix |
271	|---|---|---|---|
272	| B01 | Each game appears twice in raw data (once per team) | Model trains on duplicate data, inflates performance | Deduplicate by GAME_ID in `clean.py`, keep one row per game |
273	| B02 | `SEASON_ID` prefix `4` = playoffs, `2` = regular season | Mixing playoff and regular season games confuses rolling averages | Filter by SEASON_ID prefix before computing rolling features |
274	| B03 | `nba_api` rate limits — too many requests causes silent failures | Missing seasons in your dataset | `time.sleep(1)` between every API call. Verify row counts after each fetch. |
275	| B04 | Rolling averages at season start have insufficient history | First ~10 games of each season have NaN features | Drop rows where rolling window is incomplete OR fill with season average |
276	| B05 | Player availability from box score is approximate | Feature is noisy | Flag it as approximate in docs. Don't over-rely on it. |
277	| B06 | Current season (2025-26) only has completed games | Tonight's game won't be in the data | Re-run `fetch_current.py` before each prediction session |
278	
279	### Model Layer
280	| # | Issue | Impact | Fix |
281	|---|---|---|---|
282	| B07 | Random train/test split causes data leakage | Model looks great in eval, fails in production | Always split by season, never by random game index |
283	| B08 | XGBoost outputs uncalibrated probabilities | Probabilities are systematically overconfident | Apply isotonic regression calibration layer before serving |
284	| B09 | Class imbalance: home team wins ~58% | Model biased toward predicting home wins | Check class balance. Add `scale_pos_weight` to XGBoost if needed. |
285	
286	### API Layer
287	| # | Issue | Impact | Fix |
288	|---|---|---|---|
289	| B10 | Model loaded fresh on every request | Slow response times | Load model at app startup using FastAPI lifespan events |
290	| B11 | Team abbreviations inconsistent across seasons | KeyError on prediction | Normalize all abbreviations in `clean.py`. Maintain a mapping dict. |
291	
292	---
293	
294	## 8. Bug Tracking Protocol
295	
296	When you find a new bug during development:
297	
298	1. **Add it to the table in Section 7** with a number, description, impact, and fix.
299	2. **Mark it with status**: `OPEN`, `IN PROGRESS`, or `FIXED`.
300	3. **Never delete fixed bugs** — keep them as a record. Add a `Fix commit:` note.
301	4. **If a bug blocks you for more than 30 minutes**, write down exactly what you tried before asking for help. This forces clarity.
302	
303	Template for new bugs:
304	```
305	| B## | [What breaks] | [What goes wrong] | [How to fix it] | OPEN |
306	```
307	
308	---
309	
310	## 9. Feature Backlog
311	
312	These are ideas that are explicitly OUT OF SCOPE for v1. Do not build these until Weeks 1-8 are complete and deployed.
313	
314	| Feature | Why it's interesting | Why it's deferred |
315	|---|---|---|
316	| Automated data refresh (cron job) | Removes manual step | Adds infrastructure complexity, not needed for portfolio |
317	| LSTM / sequence model | Captures game momentum | 60-hour budget doesn't support it without sacrificing calibration |
318	| Injury report integration | Biggest signal in NBA predictions | Data is messy, unstructured, hard to get historically |
319	| Series outcome prediction | More interesting target | Requires game-level model first |
320	| Monte Carlo playoff simulator | Distribution output, not point estimate | Week 4+ complexity, build after v1 |
321	| User accounts / saved predictions | Product feature | This is a portfolio project, not a product |
322	| Postgres database | Proper persistence | CSV files are sufficient for v1 |
323	
324	---
325	
326	## 10. Code Standards
327	
328	### File organization
329	- One responsibility per file. `clean.py` cleans. `features.py` engineers features. They do not overlap.
330	- No business logic in `main.py` (the API). It calls functions from other modules.
331	- No hardcoded paths. Use a `config.py` or constants at the top of each file.
332	
333	### Python style
334	- Type hints on all function signatures
335	- Docstring on every function explaining what it takes and what it returns
336	- `logging` not `print` for anything that runs in production
337	- Constants in ALL_CAPS at the top of the file
338	
339	### Example of what good code looks like in this project:
340	```python
341	# src/data/clean.py
342	
343	import pandas as pd
344	import logging
345	from pathlib import Path
346	
347	RAW_PATH = Path("data/raw/games_raw.csv")
348	CLEAN_PATH = Path("data/processed/games_clean.csv")
349	
350	logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
351	
352	def load_raw(path: Path = RAW_PATH) -> pd.DataFrame:
353	    """Load raw game data from CSV. Raises FileNotFoundError if missing."""
354	    logger.info(f"Loading raw data from {path}")
355	    return pd.read_csv(path)
356	
357	def deduplicate_games(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
358	    """
359	    Raw data has two rows per game (one per team).
360	    Keep home team row only, identified by 'vs.' in MATCHUP column.
361	    """
362	    return df[df["MATCHUP"].str.contains("vs.")].copy()
363	
364	def add_home_away_flag(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
365	    """Add IS_HOME column: 1 if home team, 0 if away."""
366	    df["IS_HOME"] = df["MATCHUP"].str.contains("vs.").astype(int)
367	    return df
368	```
369	
370	### Git discipline
371	- Commit after every milestone, not every file save
372	- Commit messages: `feat: add rolling averages to features.py` not `update`
373	- Never commit: `.env`, `data/`, `models/`, `__pycache__/`
374	- Branch for experiments: `git checkout -b experiment/lstm` — never experiment on `main`
375	
376	---
377	
378	## 11. Evaluation Criteria
379	
380	### How to know if your model is good
381	
382	| Metric | What it measures | Target | How to compute |
383	|---|---|---|---|
384	| Accuracy | % of games predicted correctly | > 58% (beat home-team baseline) | `sklearn.metrics.accuracy_score` |
385	| Brier Score | Calibration quality (lower = better) | < 0.25 | `sklearn.metrics.brier_score_loss` |
386	| Log Loss | Confidence of correct predictions | < 0.65 | `sklearn.metrics.log_loss` |
387	| Calibration curve | Are 70% predictions right 70% of the time? | Diagonal line | `sklearn.calibration.calibration_curve` |
388	| Vegas comparison | How often does your model agree with the favorite? | Document honestly | Compare your predicted winner vs Vegas favorite |
389	
390	### What to do when results are bad
391	- Brier score > 0.25: Check for data leakage first. Then check feature quality.
392	- Calibration curve is off: Apply isotonic regression. If still off, check class balance.
393	- Worse than home-team baseline: Your features have no signal. Go back to feature engineering.
394	
395	---
396	
397	## 12. Deployment
398	
399	### API (FastAPI)
400	- Deploy to Railway: connect GitHub repo, set start command to `uvicorn src.api.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port $PORT`
401	- Set environment variables in Railway dashboard, never in code
402	- Test `/health` endpoint after every deploy
403	
404	### Frontend (Next.js)
405	- Deploy to Vercel: connect GitHub repo, set `frontend/` as root directory
406	- Set `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL` environment variable to your Railway API URL
407	
408	### After deploying
409	- Run a real prediction through the live URL
410	- Check "last updated" timestamp is showing correctly
411	- Check calibration chart renders
412

416	
417	If you're coming back after a break, do this in order:
418	
419	1. Read this file top to bottom — 10 minutes
420	2. Check which milestone you're on in Section 5
421	3. Check Section 7 for any OPEN bugs relevant to your current phase
422	4. Run `git log --oneline -10` to see what you last committed
423	5. Run the pipeline from your current phase forward to make sure nothing is broken
424	6. Only then write new code

```text
=== fetch_games.py ===
from nba_api.stats.endpoints import leaguegamefinder
import pandas as pd
import time

seasons = [
    '2025-26'
]

all_games = []

for season in seasons:
    print(f"Fetching {season}...")
    finder = leaguegamefinder.LeagueGameFinder(season_nullable=season)
    df = finder.get_data_frames()[0]
    all_games.append(df)
    time.sleep(1)  # respect rate limits

games = pd.concat(all_games, ignore_index=True)
games.to_csv('data/raw/games_current.csv', index=False)
print(f"Done. Total rows: {games.shape[0]}")=== clean.py ===
```

## Where the project actually is

PLAN.md's "Next action" line (fetch current-season data) is **already done** — the plan is stale. Real Week 1 status:

| Step | Plan | Reality |
|---|---|---|
| Install nba_api/pandas | ✅ | ✅ (`.venv` present) |
| `games_raw.csv` (historical) | ✅ | ✅ 24,589 rows, 2015–2023 |

| `clean.py` | ⬜ | ⬜ file exists but **empty (0 bytes)** ← you are here |
| Verify clean output | ⬜ | ⬜ |

So the next concrete action is **writing `clean.py`**. Two things the plan didn't anticipate that I found in the data:

1. **`SEASON_ID` has 6 prefixes, not 2.** Plan assumed `2`=regular, `4`=playoffs. Actual: `1`=preseason (1,478), `2`=regular (21,498), `3`=all-star (61), `4`=playoffs (1,500), `5`=play-in (50), `6`=in-season tournament (2). Bug B02 needs to handle all of these.
2. **A genuine contradiction in the plan.** Exit criteria (line 151) says clean output has *"one row per team per game"* (keep both rows), but bug B01 + the example code say *dedup to one row per game, home team only*. These produce different files and different downstream code.

- **One row per game (home view)** - Dedup to home-team rows only (MATCHUP contains 'vs.'), target = did home win. Matches bug B01 + the example code. Simpler for the API/predict step, but features.py must self-join to get away-team rolling stats.

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```python
"""Clean and normalize raw NBA game data.

Produces one row per team per game (two rows per game) with a home/away flag,
normalized team abbreviations, parsed dates, and a season-type label. Dedup to a
single matchup row happens later, at the feature-join step in features.py.

Run:
    python clean.py
"""

import logging
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd

RAW_PATH = Path("data/raw/games_raw.csv")
CURRENT_PATH = Path("data/raw/games_current.csv")
CLEAN_PATH = Path("data/processed/games_clean.csv")
CURRENT_CLEAN_PATH = Path("data/processed/games_current_clean.csv")

# SEASON_ID is a 5-char string: first char is the game-type prefix, last four
# are the season's starting year. Plan B02 only knew 2/4; the real data has six.
SEASON_TYPE_BY_PREFIX = {
    "1": "preseason",
    "2": "regular",
    "3": "allstar",
    "4": "playoffs",
    "5": "playin",
    "6": "tournament",
}

# Game types we keep for modeling. Preseason and all-star games are not
# competitive and carry no predictive signal, so they are dropped.
KEEP_SEASON_TYPES = {"regular", "playoffs", "playin", "tournament"}

# Bug B11: team abbreviations drift across seasons. Map historical/alternate
# codes onto each franchise's current abbreviation so joins never KeyError.
TEAM_ABBREVIATION_MAP = {
    "NOH": "NOP",  # New Orleans Hornets -> Pelicans
    "NJN": "BKN",  # New Jersey Nets -> Brooklyn
    "NOK": "NOP",  # New Orleans/Oklahoma City Hornets -> Pelicans
    "SEA": "OKC",  # Seattle SuperSonics -> Oklahoma City Thunder
    "VAN": "MEM",  # Vancouver Grizzlies -> Memphis
    "CHH": "CHA",  # Charlotte Hornets (original) -> Charlotte
    "PHX": "PHX",
}

# Columns that must be present and non-null for a row to be usable.
KEY_COLUMNS = ["GAME_ID", "GAME_DATE", "TEAM_ABBREVIATION", "MATCHUP", "WL", "PTS"]

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def load_raw(path: Path) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Load raw game data from CSV. Raises FileNotFoundError if missing."""
    logger.info("Loading raw data from %s", path)
    if not path.exists():
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"Expected raw data at {path}; run fetch_games.py first.")
    # GAME_ID and SEASON_ID are zero-padded identifiers, not numbers.
    return pd.read_csv(path, dtype={"GAME_ID": str, "SEASON_ID": str})


def add_season_type(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Add SEASON_TYPE (regular/playoffs/...), IS_PLAYOFF, and SEASON year."""
    df = df.copy()
    prefix = df["SEASON_ID"].str[0]
    df["SEASON_TYPE"] = prefix.map(SEASON_TYPE_BY_PREFIX).fillna("unknown")
    df["IS_PLAYOFF"] = (df["SEASON_TYPE"] == "playoffs").astype(int)
    df["SEASON"] = df["SEASON_ID"].str[1:].astype(int)  # starting year, e.g. 2015
    return df


def filter_game_types(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Keep only competitive game types (drop preseason and all-star)."""
    before = len(df)
    df = df[df["SEASON_TYPE"].isin(KEEP_SEASON_TYPES)].copy()
    logger.info("Filtered game types: %d -> %d rows", before, len(df))
    return df


def normalize_teams(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Map alternate/historical team abbreviations onto current ones (B11)."""
    df = df.copy()
    df["TEAM_ABBREVIATION"] = df["TEAM_ABBREVIATION"].replace(TEAM_ABBREVIATION_MAP)
    return df


def parse_dates(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Parse GAME_DATE to datetime; drop rows that fail to parse."""
    df = df.copy()
    df["GAME_DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(df["GAME_DATE"], errors="coerce")
    bad = df["GAME_DATE"].isna().sum()
    if bad:
        logger.warning("Dropping %d rows with unparseable GAME_DATE", bad)
        df = df[df["GAME_DATE"].notna()].copy()
    return df


def add_home_away(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Add IS_HOME (1 home / 0 away) and OPPONENT from the MATCHUP string.

    MATCHUP looks like "GSW vs. CLE" (home) or "GSW @ CLE" (away).
    """
    df = df.copy()
    df["IS_HOME"] = df["MATCHUP"].str.contains("vs.", regex=False).astype(int)
    # Opponent is the token after "vs." or "@".
    df["OPPONENT"] = (
        df["MATCHUP"].str.split(r"\s+(?:vs\.|@)\s+", regex=True).str[-1].str.strip()
    )
    df["OPPONENT"] = df["OPPONENT"].replace(TEAM_ABBREVIATION_MAP)
    return df


def drop_bad_rows(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Drop rows with nulls in key columns (B-data integrity)."""
    before = len(df)
    df = df.dropna(subset=KEY_COLUMNS).copy()
    dropped = before - len(df)
    if dropped:
        logger.warning("Dropped %d rows with nulls in key columns", dropped)
    return df


def keep_complete_games(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Keep only games that have both team rows (exactly 2 rows per GAME_ID)."""
    counts = df.groupby("GAME_ID")["GAME_ID"].transform("size")
    complete = df[counts == 2].copy()
    dropped_games = (counts != 2).sum()
    if dropped_games:
        logger.warning("Dropped %d rows from games without exactly 2 team rows", dropped_games)
    return complete


def clean(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Run the full cleaning pipeline on a raw games DataFrame."""
    df = add_season_type(df)
    df = filter_game_types(df)
    df = normalize_teams(df)
    df = parse_dates(df)
    df = add_home_away(df)
    df = drop_bad_rows(df)
    df = keep_complete_games(df)
    df = df.sort_values(["GAME_DATE", "GAME_ID", "IS_HOME"]).reset_index(drop=True)
    return df


def verify(df: pd.DataFrame) -> None:
    """Assert the Week-1 exit criteria hold for cleaned data."""
    assert df["WL"].isna().sum() == 0, "WL has nulls"
    assert df["GAME_DATE"].notna().all(), "GAME_DATE has unparsed values"
    assert (df.groupby("GAME_ID").size() == 2).all(), "not exactly 2 rows per game"
    assert set(df["IS_HOME"].unique()) <= {0, 1}, "IS_HOME not binary"
    logger.info(
        "Verified: %d games, %d rows, seasons %d-%d, types %s",
        df["GAME_ID"].nunique(),
        len(df),
        df["SEASON"].min(),
        df["SEASON"].max(),
        sorted(df["SEASON_TYPE"].unique()),
    )


def run(src: Path, dst: Path) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Clean one raw file and write the result to dst."""
    df = clean(load_raw(src))
    verify(df)
    dst.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    df.to_csv(dst, index=False)
    logger.info("Wrote %d rows to %s", len(df), dst)
    return df


def main() -> None:
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format="%(levelname)s %(message)s")
    run(RAW_PATH, CLEAN_PATH)
    if CURRENT_PATH.exists():
        run(CURRENT_PATH, CURRENT_CLEAN_PATH)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

```

</details></tool-use>

```python
def clean(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:

    verify(df)
    dst.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    df.to_csv(dst, index=False)
    logger.info("Wrote %d rows to %s", len(df), dst)
    return df


def main() -> None:
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format="%(levelname)s %(message)s")
    run(RAW_PATH, CLEAN_PATH)
    if CURRENT_PATH.exists():
        run(CURRENT_PATH, CURRENT_CLEAN_PATH)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```python
"""Clean and normalize raw NBA game data.

Produces one row per team per game (two rows per game) with a home/away flag,
normalized team abbreviations, parsed dates, and a season-type label. Dedup to a
single matchup row happens later, at the feature-join step in features.py.

Run:
    python clean.py
"""

import logging
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd

RAW_PATH = Path("data/raw/games_raw.csv")
CURRENT_PATH = Path("data/raw/games_current.csv")
CLEAN_PATH = Path("data/processed/games_clean.csv")
CURRENT_CLEAN_PATH = Path("data/processed/games_current_clean.csv")

# SEASON_ID is a 5-char string: first char is the game-type prefix, last four
# are the season's starting year. Plan B02 only knew 2/4; the real data has six.
SEASON_TYPE_BY_PREFIX = {
    "1": "preseason",
    "2": "regular",
    "3": "allstar",
    "4": "playoffs",
    "5": "playin",
    "6": "tournament",
}

# Game types we keep for modeling. Preseason and all-star games are not
# competitive and carry no predictive signal, so they are dropped.
KEEP_SEASON_TYPES = {"regular", "playoffs", "playin", "tournament"}

# Bug B11: team abbreviations drift across seasons. Map historical/alternate
# codes onto each franchise's current abbreviation so joins never KeyError.
TEAM_ABBREVIATION_MAP = {
    "NOH": "NOP",  # New Orleans Hornets -> Pelicans
    "NJN": "BKN",  # New Jersey Nets -> Brooklyn
    "NOK": "NOP",  # New Orleans/Oklahoma City Hornets -> Pelicans
    "SEA": "OKC",  # Seattle SuperSonics -> Oklahoma City Thunder
    "VAN": "MEM",  # Vancouver Grizzlies -> Memphis
    "CHH": "CHA",  # Charlotte Hornets (original) -> Charlotte
}

# Columns that must be present and non-null for a row to be usable.
KEY_COLUMNS = ["GAME_ID", "GAME_DATE", "TEAM_ABBREVIATION", "MATCHUP", "WL", "PTS"]

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def load_raw(path: Path) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Load raw game data from CSV. Raises FileNotFoundError if missing."""
    logger.info("Loading raw data from %s", path)
    if not path.exists():
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"Expected raw data at {path}; run fetch_games.py first.")
    # GAME_ID and SEASON_ID are zero-padded identifiers, not numbers.
    return pd.read_csv(path, dtype={"GAME_ID": str, "SEASON_ID": str})


def add_season_type(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Add SEASON_TYPE (regular/playoffs/...), IS_PLAYOFF, and SEASON year."""
    df = df.copy()
    prefix = df["SEASON_ID"].str[0]
    df["SEASON_TYPE"] = prefix.map(SEASON_TYPE_BY_PREFIX).fillna("unknown")
    df["IS_PLAYOFF"] = (df["SEASON_TYPE"] == "playoffs").astype(int)
    df["SEASON"] = df["SEASON_ID"].str[1:].astype(int)  # starting year, e.g. 2015
    return df


def filter_game_types(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Keep only competitive game types (drop preseason and all-star)."""
    before = len(df)
    df = df[df["SEASON_TYPE"].isin(KEEP_SEASON_TYPES)].copy()
    logger.info("Filtered game types: %d -> %d rows", before, len(df))
    return df


def normalize_teams(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Map alternate/historical team abbreviations onto current ones (B11)."""
    df = df.copy()
    df["TEAM_ABBREVIATION"] = df["TEAM_ABBREVIATION"].replace(TEAM_ABBREVIATION_MAP)
    return df


def parse_dates(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Parse GAME_DATE to datetime; drop rows that fail to parse."""
    df = df.copy()
    df["GAME_DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(df["GAME_DATE"], errors="coerce")
    bad = df["GAME_DATE"].isna().sum()
    if bad:
        logger.warning("Dropping %d rows with unparseable GAME_DATE", bad)
        df = df[df["GAME_DATE"].notna()].copy()
    return df


def add_home_away(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Add IS_HOME (1 home / 0 away) and OPPONENT from the MATCHUP string.

    MATCHUP looks like "GSW vs. CLE" (home) or "GSW @ CLE" (away).
    """
    df = df.copy()
    df["IS_HOME"] = df["MATCHUP"].str.contains("vs.", regex=False).astype(int)
    # Opponent is the token after "vs." or "@".
    df["OPPONENT"] = (
        df["MATCHUP"].str.split(r"\s+(?:vs\.|@)\s+", regex=True).str[-1].str.strip()
    )
    df["OPPONENT"] = df["OPPONENT"].replace(TEAM_ABBREVIATION_MAP)
    return df


def drop_bad_rows(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Drop rows with nulls in key columns (data integrity)."""
    before = len(df)
    df = df.dropna(subset=KEY_COLUMNS).copy()
    dropped = before - len(df)
    if dropped:
        logger.warning("Dropped %d rows with nulls in key columns", dropped)
    return df


def keep_complete_games(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Keep only games that have both team rows (exactly 2 rows per GAME_ID)."""
    counts = df.groupby("GAME_ID")["GAME_ID"].transform("size")
    complete = df[counts == 2].copy()
    dropped = (counts != 2).sum()
    if dropped:
        logger.warning("Dropped %d rows from games without exactly 2 team rows", dropped)
    return complete


def clean(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Run the full cleaning pipeline on a raw games DataFrame."""
    df = add_season_type(df)
    df = filter_game_types(df)
    df = normalize_teams(df)
    df = parse_dates(df)
    df = add_home_away(df)
    df = drop_bad_rows(df)
    df = keep_complete_games(df)
    df = df.sort_values(["GAME_DATE", "GAME_ID", "IS_HOME"]).reset_index(drop=True)
    return df


def verify(df: pd.DataFrame) -> None:
    """Assert the Week-1 exit criteria hold for cleaned data."""
    assert df["WL"].isna().sum() == 0, "WL has nulls"
    assert df["GAME_DATE"].notna().all(), "GAME_DATE has unparsed values"
    assert (df.groupby("GAME_ID").size() == 2).all(), "not exactly 2 rows per game"
    assert set(df["IS_HOME"].unique()) <= {0, 1}, "IS_HOME not binary"
    logger.info(
        "Verified: %d games, %d rows, seasons %d-%d, types %s",
        df["GAME_ID"].nunique(),
        len(df),
        df["SEASON"].min(),
        df["SEASON"].max(),
        sorted(df["SEASON_TYPE"].unique()),
    )


def run(src: Path, dst: Path) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Clean one raw file and write the result to dst."""
    df = clean(load_raw(src))
    verify(df)
    dst.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    df.to_csv(dst, index=False)
    logger.info("Wrote %d rows to %s", len(df), dst)
    return df


def main() -> None:
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format="%(levelname)s %(message)s")
    run(RAW_PATH, CLEAN_PATH)
    if CURRENT_PATH.exists():
        run(CURRENT_PATH, CURRENT_CLEAN_PATH)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

```

```python
"""Clean and normalize raw NBA game data.

Produces one row per team per game (two rows per game) with a home/away flag,
normalized team abbreviations, parsed dates, and a season-type label. Dedup to a
single matchup row happens later, at the feature-join step in features.py.

Run:
    python clean.py
"""

import logging
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd

RAW_PATH = Path("data/raw/games_raw.csv")
CURRENT_PATH = Path("data/raw/games_current.csv")
CLEAN_PATH = Path("data/processed/games_clean.csv")
CURRENT_CLEAN_PATH = Path("data/processed/games_current_clean.csv")

# SEASON_ID is a 5-char string: first char is the game-type prefix, last four
# are the season's starting year. Plan B02 only knew 2/4; the real data has six.
SEASON_TYPES_BY_PREFIX = {
    "1": "preseason",
    "2": "regular_season",
    "3": "allstar",
    "4": "playoffs",
    "5": "playin",
    "6": "tournament",
}

# Preseason and all-star games are not competitive, so we drop them.
KEEP_SEASON_TYPES = {"regular_season", "playoffs", "playin", "tournament"}

# Bug B11: team abbreviations drift across seasons. Map historical/alternate
# codes onto each franchise's current abbreviation so joins never KeyError.
TEAM_ABBREVIATION_MAP = {
    "NOH": "NOP",  # New Orleans Hornets -> Pelicans
    "NJN": "BKN",  # New Jersey Nets -> Brooklyn
    "NOK": "NOP",  # New Orleans/Oklahoma City Hornets -> Pelicans
    "SEA": "OKC",  # Seattle SuperSonics -> Oklahoma City Thunder
    "VAN": "MEM",  # Vancouver Grizzlies -> Memphis
    "CHH": "CHA",  # Charlotte Hornets (original) -> Charlotte
}

# Columns that must be present and non-null for a row to be usable.
KEY_COLUMNS = ["GAME_ID", "GAME_DATE", "TEAM_ABBREVIATION", "MATCHUP", "WL", "PTS"]

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def load_raw(path: Path) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Load raw game data from CSV. Raises FileNotFoundError if missing."""
    logger.info("Loading raw data from %s", path)
    if not path.exists():
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"Expected raw data at {path}; run fetch_games.py first.")
    # GAME_ID and SEASON_ID are zero-padded identifiers, not numbers.
    return pd.read_csv(path, dtype={"GAME_ID": str, "SEASON_ID": str})


def add_season_type(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Add SEASON_TYPE (regular_season/playoffs/...), IS_PLAYOFF, and SEASON year."""
    df = df.copy()
    prefix = df["SEASON_ID"].str[0]
    df["SEASON_TYPE"] = prefix.map(SEASON_TYPES_BY_PREFIX).fillna("unknown")
    df["IS_PLAYOFF"] = (df["SEASON_TYPE"] == "playoffs").astype(int)
    df["SEASON"] = df["SEASON_ID"].str[1:].astype(int)  # starting year, e.g. 2015
    return df


def filter_game_types(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Keep only competitive game types (drop preseason and all-star)."""
    before = len(df)
    df = df[df["SEASON_TYPE"].isin(KEEP_SEASON_TYPES)].copy()
    logger.info("Filtered game types: %d -> %d rows", before, len(df))
    return df


def normalize_teams(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Map alternate/historical team abbreviations onto current ones (B11)."""
    df = df.copy()
    df["TEAM_ABBREVIATION"] = df["TEAM_ABBREVIATION"].replace(TEAM_ABBREVIATION_MAP)
    return df


def parse_dates(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Parse GAME_DATE to datetime; drop rows that fail to parse."""
    df = df.copy()
    df["GAME_DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(df["GAME_DATE"], errors="coerce")
    bad = df["GAME_DATE"].isna().sum()
    if bad:
        logger.warning("Dropping %d rows with unparseable GAME_DATE", bad)
        df = df[df["GAME_DATE"].notna()].copy()
    return df


def add_home_away(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Add IS_HOME (1 home / 0 away) and OPPONENT from the MATCHUP string.

    MATCHUP looks like "GSW vs. CLE" (home) or "GSW @ CLE" (away).
    """
    df = df.copy()
    df["IS_HOME"] = df["MATCHUP"].str.contains("vs.", regex=False).astype(int)
    # Opponent is the token after "vs." or "@".
    df["OPPONENT"] = (
        df["MATCHUP"].str.split(r"\s+(?:vs\.|@)\s+", regex=True).str[-1].str.strip()
    )
    df["OPPONENT"] = df["OPPONENT"].replace(TEAM_ABBREVIATION_MAP)
    return df


def drop_bad_rows(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Drop rows with nulls in key columns (data integrity)."""
    before = len(df)
    df = df.dropna(subset=KEY_COLUMNS).copy()
    dropped = before - len(df)
    if dropped:
        logger.warning("Dropped %d rows with nulls in key columns", dropped)
    return df


def keep_complete_games(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Keep only games that have both team rows (exactly 2 rows per GAME_ID)."""
    counts = df.groupby("GAME_ID")["GAME_ID"].transform("size")
    complete = df[counts == 2].copy()
    dropped = (counts != 2).sum()
    if dropped:
        logger.warning("Dropped %d rows from games without exactly 2 team rows", dropped)
    return complete


def clean(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Run the full cleaning pipeline on a raw games DataFrame."""
    df = df.drop_duplicates()
    df = add_season_type(df)
    df = filter_game_types(df)
    df = normalize_teams(df)
    df = parse_dates(df)
    df = add_home_away(df)
    df = drop_bad_rows(df)
    df = keep_complete_games(df)
    df = df.sort_values(["GAME_DATE", "GAME_ID", "IS_HOME"]).reset_index(drop=True)
    return df


def verify(df: pd.DataFrame) -> None:
    """Assert the Week-1 exit criteria hold for cleaned data."""
    assert df["WL"].isna().sum() == 0, "WL has nulls"
    assert df["GAME_DATE"].notna().all(), "GAME_DATE has unparsed values"
    assert (df.groupby("GAME_ID").size() == 2).all(), "not exactly 2 rows per game"
    assert set(df["IS_HOME"].unique()) <= {0, 1}, "IS_HOME not binary"
    logger.info(
        "Verified: %d games, %d rows, seasons %d-%d, types %s",
        df["GAME_ID"].nunique(),
        len(df),
        df["SEASON"].min(),
        df["SEASON"].max(),
        sorted(df["SEASON_TYPE"].unique()),
    )


def run(src: Path, dst: Path) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Clean one raw file and write the result to dst."""
    df = clean(load_raw(src))
    verify(df)
    dst.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    df.to_csv(dst, index=False)
    logger.info("Wrote %d rows to %s", len(df), dst)
    return df


def main() -> None:
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format="%(levelname)s %(message)s")
    run(RAW_PATH, CLEAN_PATH)
    if CURRENT_PATH.exists():
        run(CURRENT_PATH, CURRENT_CLEAN_PATH)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

```

## 1. Section 5 — Week 2 (lines 153–165): the task list

```
### Week 2 — Feature Engineering
- [ ] Write features.py
- [ ] Rolling 10-game averages per team (PTS, REB, AST, FG_PCT, PLUS_MINUS)
- [ ] Rest days feature (days since last game per team)
- [ ] Home/away binary flag
- [ ] Player availability flag (approximate from box score: did starters play? check MIN)
- [ ] Season-based train/val/test split:
  - Train: 2015-16 to 2021-22
  - Val: 2022-23
  - Test: 2023-24 to 2024-25

```

**Exit criteria (line 165):** *"`features.csv` exists. Train/val/test row counts verified. No future data leaks into training set."*

## 2. Other references

- **Architecture (line 70):** `features.py` → *"Engineers features from clean data"*, output `data/processed/features.csv` (line 57: *"Engineered features ready for modeling"*).
- **Code standards (line 329):** *"`features.py` engineers features. They do not overlap [with clean.py]."*

## Relevant gotchas it must handle (Section 7)

| Bug | What `features.py` has to do |
|---|---|
| **B04** (line 275) | First ~10 games of each season have NaN rolling features → drop incomplete-window rows OR fill with season average |
| **B07** (line 282) | Always split by season, **never** random game index (data leakage) |
| **B02** (line 273) | Filter by season type before computing rolling averages so playoffs/regular don't bleed together |

## So, in plain terms — what `features.py` does

Takes `games_clean.csv` (one row per team per game) and produces `features.csv` where each row is a **matchup** with features for both teams as of *before* that game:

1. **Rolling 10-game averages** per team: PTS, REB, AST, FG_PCT, PLUS_MINUS — shifted so the current game isn't included (no leakage).
2. **Rest days** — days since each team's previous game.
3. **Home/away flag** — already in clean data as `IS_HOME`.
4. **Player-availability flag** — approximate, from MIN in box score (noisy; B05 says flag it as approximate).
5. **Target** — did the home team win.
6. **Season-based train/val/test split** column (or three files).

## One thing to flag before I start

The plan's split (lines 161–162) says **Test: 2023-24 to 2024-25**, but your historical data only goes through **2023** (`games_clean.csv` is seasons 2015–2023). So the test set as written would be nearly empty. Your options when I build it:

- **A:** Shift the split to fit actual data (e.g. Train 2015–2020, Val 2021, Test 2022–2023).
- **B:** Re-fetch historical data to include 2024-25 before building features.
- **C:** Use the 2025-26 `games_current_clean.csv` as the test set.

```python
"""Fetch historical NBA games (2015-16 through 2024-25) into games_raw.csv.

One LeagueGameFinder call per season, with a sleep between calls to respect
nba_api rate limits (Bug B03). Each season returns two rows per game (one per
team) and all game types; cleaning/filtering happens later in clean.py.

Run:
    python fetch_games.py
"""

import time

import pandas as pd
from nba_api.stats.endpoints import leaguegamefinder

# Historical training range. Current season (2025-26) is fetched separately by
# fetch_current.py into games_current.csv.
SEASONS = [
    "2015-16",
    "2016-17",
    "2017-18",
    "2018-19",
    "2019-20",
    "2020-21",
    "2021-22",
    "2022-23",
    "2023-24",
    "2024-25",
]

OUTPUT_PATH = "data/raw/games_raw.csv"


def fetch_seasons(seasons: list[str]) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Fetch all games for each season and return them concatenated."""
    all_games = []
    for season in seasons:
        print(f"Fetching {season}...")
        finder = leaguegamefinder.LeagueGameFinder(season_nullable=season)
        df = finder.get_data_frames()[0]
        print(f"  {len(df)} rows")
        all_games.append(df)
        time.sleep(1)  # respect rate limits (B03)
    return pd.concat(all_games, ignore_index=True)


def main() -> None:
    games = fetch_seasons(SEASONS)
    games.to_csv(OUTPUT_PATH, index=False)
    print(f"Done. Wrote {games.shape[0]} rows to {OUTPUT_PATH}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

```

```python
"""Fetch the current NBA season (2025-26) into games_current.csv.

Run this before each prediction session so tonight's completed games are
included (Bug B06). Same shape as fetch_games.py, just a different season set
and output file.

Run:
    python fetch_current.py
"""

import time

import pandas as pd
from nba_api.stats.endpoints import leaguegamefinder

SEASONS = ["2025-26"]
OUTPUT_PATH = "data/raw/games_current.csv"


def fetch_seasons(seasons: list[str]) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Fetch all games for each season and return them concatenated."""
    all_games = []
    for season in seasons:
        print(f"Fetching {season}...")
        finder = leaguegamefinder.LeagueGameFinder(season_nullable=season)
        df = finder.get_data_frames()[0]
        print(f"  {len(df)} rows")
        all_games.append(df)
        time.sleep(1)  # respect rate limits (B03)
    return pd.concat(all_games, ignore_index=True)


def main() -> None:
    games = fetch_seasons(SEASONS)
    games.to_csv(OUTPUT_PATH, index=False)
    print(f"Done. Wrote {games.shape[0]} rows to {OUTPUT_PATH}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

```

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```python
"""Engineer matchup features from cleaned game data.

Input  : data/processed/games_clean.csv (one row per team per game)
Output : data/processed/features.csv      (one row per game = one matchup)

Each output row describes a single game with each team's form measured strictly
*before* that game (no leakage, Bug B07): rolling 10-game averages, rest days,
and a home/away framing. Target is HOME_WIN. A SPLIT column assigns each game to
train/val/test by season so the modeling step never splits randomly.

Run:
    python features.py
"""

import logging
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd

CLEAN_PATH = Path("data/processed/games_clean.csv")
FEATURES_PATH = Path("data/processed/features.csv")

# Box-score stats we average over a rolling window per team.
ROLLING_STATS = ["PTS", "REB", "AST", "FG_PCT", "PLUS_MINUS"]
ROLLING_WINDOW = 10

# Season-based split (Bug B07: never split randomly across games). SEASON is the
# starting year, e.g. 2021 == the 2021-22 season.
TRAIN_SEASONS = range(2015, 2022)  # 2015-16 .. 2021-22
VAL_SEASONS = {2022}               # 2022-23
TEST_SEASONS = {2023, 2024}        # 2023-24 .. 2024-25

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def load_clean(path: Path = CLEAN_PATH) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Load cleaned per-team game rows. Raises FileNotFoundError if missing."""
    logger.info("Loading clean data from %s", path)
    if not path.exists():
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"Expected clean data at {path}; run clean.py first.")
    df = pd.read_csv(path, dtype={"GAME_ID": str, "SEASON_ID": str}, parse_dates=["GAME_DATE"])
    return df.sort_values(["TEAM_ABBREVIATION", "GAME_DATE", "GAME_ID"]).reset_index(drop=True)


def add_rest_days(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Add REST_DAYS: days since this team's previous game in the same season.

    Rest is physical, so it spans game types within a season. The first game of
    a team's season has no prior game and is left as NaN (dropped downstream).
    """
    df = df.copy()
    prev_date = df.groupby(["TEAM_ABBREVIATION", "SEASON"])["GAME_DATE"].shift(1)
    df["REST_DAYS"] = (df["GAME_DATE"] - prev_date).dt.days
    return df


def add_rolling_features(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Add ROLL_<stat>: mean of the prior ROLLING_WINDOW games for that team.

    Computed per (SEASON, SEASON_TYPE, team) so playoff and regular-season form
    don't bleed together (Bug B02), and shifted by one game so the current game
    is never included (Bug B07). Requires a full window; partial windows are NaN
    and dropped later (Bug B04).
    """
    df = df.copy()
    grp = df.groupby(["SEASON", "SEASON_TYPE", "TEAM_ABBREVIATION"])
    for stat in ROLLING_STATS:
        df[f"ROLL_{stat}"] = grp[stat].transform(
            lambda s: s.shift(1).rolling(ROLLING_WINDOW, min_periods=ROLLING_WINDOW).mean()
        )
    return df


def build_matchups(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Pivot two team-rows per game into one matchup row (home vs away)."""
    feature_cols = ["REST_DAYS"] + [f"ROLL_{s}" for s in ROLLING_STATS]
    keep = ["GAME_ID", "GAME_DATE", "SEASON", "SEASON_TYPE", "TEAM_ABBREVIATION", "WL"] + feature_cols

    home = df[df["IS_HOME"] == 1][keep].copy()
    away = df[df["IS_HOME"] == 0][keep].copy()

    home = home.rename(columns={"TEAM_ABBREVIATION": "HOME_TEAM"})
    away = away.rename(columns={"TEAM_ABBREVIATION": "AWAY_TEAM"})
    home = home.rename(columns={c: f"HOME_{c}" for c in feature_cols})
    away = away.rename(columns={c: f"AWAY_{c}" for c in feature_cols})

    # Game-level fields come from the home row; merge away features on GAME_ID.
    away_keep = ["GAME_ID", "AWAY_TEAM"] + [f"AWAY_{c}" for c in feature_cols]
    matchups = home.merge(away[away_keep], on="GAME_ID", how="inner")

    matchups["HOME_WIN"] = (matchups["WL"] == "W").astype(int)
    matchups = matchups.drop(columns=["WL"])
    return matchups


def assign_split(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Add SPLIT (train/val/test) by season; drop seasons outside all splits."""
    df = df.copy()

    def which(season: int) -> str:
        if season in TRAIN_SEASONS:
            return "train"
        if season in VAL_SEASONS:
            return "val"
        if season in TEST_SEASONS:
            return "test"
        return "none"

    df["SPLIT"] = df["SEASON"].map(which)
    dropped = (df["SPLIT"] == "none").sum()
    if dropped:
        logger.warning("Dropping %d games from seasons outside train/val/test", dropped)
    return df[df["SPLIT"] != "none"].copy()


def engineer(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Run the full feature pipeline on cleaned game rows."""
    df = add_rest_days(df)
    df = add_rolling_features(df)
    matchups = build_matchups(df)

    feature_cols = [c for c in matchups.columns if c.startswith(("HOME_ROLL", "AWAY_ROLL", "HOME_REST", "AWAY_REST"))]
    before = len(matchups)
    matchups = matchups.dropna(subset=feature_cols).copy()  # B04: incomplete windows
    logger.info("Dropped %d matchups with incomplete feature windows", before - len(matchups))

    matchups = assign_split(matchups)
    matchups = matchups.sort_values(["GAME_DATE", "GAME_ID"]).reset_index(drop=True)
    return matchups


def verify(df: pd.DataFrame) -> None:
    """Sanity-check the engineered features and report split counts."""
    feature_cols = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith(("HOME_", "AWAY_")) and c not in ("HOME_TEAM", "AWAY_TEAM")]
    assert df[feature_cols].isna().sum().sum() == 0, "NaNs remain in feature columns"
    assert df["GAME_ID"].is_unique, "duplicate games in feature matrix"
    assert set(df["HOME_WIN"].unique()) <= {0, 1}, "HOME_WIN not binary"

    counts = df["SPLIT"].value_counts().to_dict()
    logger.info("Split counts: %s", {k: counts.get(k, 0) for k in ("train", "val", "test")})
    logger.info("Home win rate: %.3f over %d games", df["HOME_WIN"].mean(), len(df))


def main() -> None:
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format="%(levelname)s %(message)s")
    features = engineer(load_clean())
    verify(features)
    FEATURES_PATH.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    features.to_csv(FEATURES_PATH, index=False)
    logger.info("Wrote %d rows, %d columns to %s", len(features), features.shape[1], FEATURES_PATH)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

```


Done. Week 2 (Feature Engineering) is complete and the full pipeline runs clean.

## What happened

**Re-fetched (B):** `fetch_games.py` now pulls all 10 seasons 2015-16 → 2024-25 → `games_raw.csv` (27,391 rows). Split the current-season fetch into `fetch_current.py` to match the plan's structure.

**Pipeline ran end-to-end:**
- `clean.py` → `games_clean.csv`: 12,846 games, seasons 2015–2024
- `features.py` → `features.csv`: **10,616 matchups, 20 columns**

## `features.csv` — one row per game

| Group | Columns |
|---|---|
| Keys | `GAME_ID, GAME_DATE, SEASON, SEASON_TYPE` |
| Teams | `HOME_TEAM, AWAY_TEAM` |
| Rolling 10-game (shifted, no leakage — B07) | `HOME/AWAY_ROLL_{PTS,REB,AST,FG_PCT,PLUS_MINUS}` |
| Rest | `HOME/AWAY_REST_DAYS` |
| Target | `HOME_WIN` |
| Split | `SPLIT` (train/val/test) |

**Splits now fit real data:** train 7,339 (2015–2021) · val 1,094 (2022) · test 2,183 (2023–2024). Home win rate 0.566 — matches the ~58% baseline in the plan, a good sanity check.

## Decisions I made (worth confirming)

1. **Player-availability feature dropped** — needs player-level minutes; `leaguegamefinder` only gives team-aggregate `MIN`. Left out of v1 rather than faked.
2. **Rolling averages computed per (season, season-type, team)** so playoff/regular form don't mix (B02). Incomplete windows dropped (B04) — that's the 2,224 dropped matchups (first ~10 games each team-season).
3. **`SEASON_TYPE` kept in features** so `train.py` can filter to regular-season-only if you want.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-06-08 18:41:57Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-06-08 18:42:21Z)**_

Good question to ask before modeling. Here's every column in `features.csv` and exactly what job it does. I'll group them by role, because not all of them are predictors — some are bookkeeping, one is the answer the model learns to guess.

## 1. Identifiers / bookkeeping — *not fed to the model*

These exist so you can trace, join, and split data. They are **excluded** from training (a model that "learns" from a game ID is memorizing, not predicting).

| Column | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `GAME_ID` | Unique key for the game. Lets you join back to raw data, dedupe, and debug a specific prediction. |
| `GAME_DATE` | When the game happened. Used to order games chronologically so rolling stats only look backward, and to compute rest days. Not a predictor itself. |
| `SEASON` | Which season (starting year). Drives the train/val/test split so you never test on the past. |
| `SEASON_TYPE` | regular / playoffs / play-in / tournament. Lets `train.py` optionally filter (e.g. train on regular season only) and keeps rolling stats from mixing game types. |
| `HOME_TEAM` / `AWAY_TEAM` | The matchup labels. Used for display and joins. **Not** fed as raw strings — team identity isn't a predictor here (we predict from *form*, not *which logo*). |

## 2. The predictors — *what the model actually uses*

Every feature here answers one question: **"Going into this game, which team is in better shape?"** Each is computed for both HOME and AWAY, so the model can compare the two sides.

### Rolling 10-game averages (form / quality)
Each is the team's average over its **previous 10 games** — a snapshot of how well they've been playing *recently*, not season-long reputation. Computed shifted so tonight's result is never included (that would be cheating).

| Feature | What signal it carries |
|---|---|
| `ROLL_PTS` | Offensive output — how many points the team typically scores. High = strong offense. |
| `ROLL_REB` | Rebounding — extra possessions and second-chance points. Proxy for size/effort/control of the boards. |
| `ROLL_AST` | Ball movement / offensive cohesion. Teams sharing the ball tend to score more efficiently. |
| `ROLL_FG_PCT` | Shooting **efficiency** (not volume). A team scoring 110 on 50% is healthier than 110 on 40%. Captures shot quality. |
| `ROLL_PLUS_MINUS` | **The single most important one.** Average point differential — how much they win/lose *by*. A team can score a lot but lose; plus/minus captures net dominance directly. This is your best "who's better" signal. |

Why both teams: the model compares `HOME_ROLL_PLUS_MINUS` vs `AWAY_ROLL_PLUS_MINUS` etc. The *gap* between the two sides is what predicts the winner.

### Rest

| Feature | What signal it carries |
|---|---|
| `HOME_REST_DAYS` / `AWAY_REST_DAYS` | Days since that team's last game. Captures fatigue. 0 days = back-to-back (teams play worse, especially defensively). A rested team vs a tired one is a real, well-documented edge in the NBA. |

### Home advantage — *implicit, via framing*
There's no explicit "home flag" column, and that's deliberate: **every row is already written from the home team's perspective** (`HOME_WIN` is the target). The home-court edge (~57% win rate) is baked into the target framing — the model learns "home teams win ~57% of the time, adjust up or down based on the form gap." That's why the home win rate (0.566) is your baseline to beat.

## 3. The target — *the answer the model learns to predict*

| Column | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `HOME_WIN` | 1 if the home team won, 0 if not. This is the label. The model's whole job: given all the features above, output P(HOME_WIN = 1). |

## 4. Split control

| Column | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `SPLIT` | train / val / test, assigned by season. **train** = model learns patterns; **val** = tune and check Brier score; **test** = final honest evaluation on seasons it never saw. Splitting by season (not randomly) is what makes the evaluation trustworthy — in the real world you always predict the *future* from the *past*. |

---

### The one-sentence summary
The model takes **two teams' recent form** (rolling scoring, efficiency, rebounding, ball movement, and net point differential) plus **how rested each is**, framed from the home team's side, and learns to output the probability the home team wins — checked against seasons it was never trained on.

### Worth knowing before Week 3
- `ROLL_PLUS_MINUS` will almost certainly be your strongest feature; `ROLL_FG_PCT` and rest days are secondary signals.
- We have **no injury, no Vegas line, no player-level data** — so the model predicts from team form only. That's the documented ceiling (the plan already says it won't beat Vegas).

Want me to move on to `train.py` now, or adjust the feature set first (e.g. add explicit *difference* features like `PLUS_MINUS_DIFF = HOME − AWAY`, which often help tree models)?

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-06-08 18:43:57Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-06-08 18:44:10Z)**_

Deferring to v2 is exactly the right call — and you're right that it's the single biggest missing signal. Let me be honest about *why* it's genuinely more work than it sounds, so the v2 scope is clear.

## The hassle isn't "getting player stats" — it's three separate problems

**1. Player availability (medium effort, doable)**
`nba_api` *can* give per-player minutes per game (`BoxScoreTraditionalV2`, `PlayerGameLogs`). But it's **one API call per game**, so building history means ~12,000+ throttled calls (hours of fetching, rate-limit babysitting per B03) versus the 10 calls we do now. Mechanically fine, just slow and a new data layer to clean and store.

**2. Injuries specifically (this is the hard part)**
Here's the catch that trips up most NBA models:

- *Historically*, you can only infer "player was out" retroactively — they played 0 minutes / DNP. That's a backward-looking proxy, fine for **training**.
- *For predicting tonight's game*, you need to know who's out **before tip-off** — which `nba_api` does **not** provide. You'd need a live source: the official NBA injury report (PDF/scrape) or a paid feed. The training data and the prediction-time data come from *different pipelines*, which is the real engineering cost.

**3. The signal that actually matters (the deep part)**
"A player is injured" alone isn't enough — the model needs to know *how much that player matters*. LeBron out ≠ a benchwarmer out. That requires a **player-impact metric** (on/off, RAPM, EPM, or even just minutes-weighted plus/minus) layered on top of availability. Without it, an injury flag is noise.

## My recommendation: v2, in two stages

| Stage | What | Effort | Payoff |
|---|---|---|---|
| **v2a** | Availability + a simple player-value weight, used as a *training* feature | Medium | Real accuracy gain on historical eval |
| **v2b** | Live injury-report ingestion at prediction time | Higher | Makes tonight's predictions injury-aware |

This is already consistent with your plan — **PLAN.md Section 9 lists "Injury report integration"** as deferred for exactly these reasons ("data is messy, unstructured, hard to get historically"). So you're not cutting a corner; you're following the plan.

For **v1**, team rolling form already partially absorbs injuries indirectly: if a star's been out the last few games, the team's `ROLL_PLUS_MINUS` already sagged. It's a weak, lagged proxy — but it's not nothing.

Want me to record this v2 scope decision so it's captured, and then proceed to `train.py`? I'll save the decision and we keep v1 moving.
